bibliographic.title: Lydgate's Reson and Sensuallyte

dateStatements.label: Publication
dateStatements.notes: Part II bears 1903 on its title page; its preface is signed Munich, May 1904.

entities.description: Part I supplies the manuscript descriptions, edited text, Furnivall side-notes, and glossary; Part II supplies studies and notes. The Part II preface is dated Munich, May 1904, but its title page is 1903.
entities.label: Ernst Sieper, Lydgate's Reson and Sensuallyte, EETS Extra Series 84 and 89 (1901-1903)

rights.basis: Publication dates 1901 and 1903. Provider requests attached to particular scans remain separate.

entities.description: One unfinished 7,042-line Middle English verse translation/adaptation, cautiously attributed to John Lydgate.
entities.label: Reson and Sensuallyte (abstract Middle English work)
notes: The catalog_description type is the schema representation of the abstract work, not a modern catalog record.
John Lydgate remains an attributed translator-adapter, not an unqualified author.

rights.basis: Medieval historical text; no claim is extended to modern editions, markup, catalog prose, or photographs.

dateStatements.label: Production
dateStatements.value: likely written in the 1440s, possibly completed in 1450

entities.description: Principal witness used by Sieper; a mid-fifteenth-century English parchment codex. The exact work starts on f. 202r and ends on f. 300r.
entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Fairfax 16, ff. 202r-300r
notes: The first XML folio milestone is 202v after 39 poem lines, but direct image inspection proves that the work begins on f. 202r.
Reson and Sensuallyte occupies ff. 202r-300r.

rights.basis: Copyright status is not assigned to the physical medieval carrier; historical-text and digital-image rights are recorded on their own entities.

reviewNotes: The final graph has 21 entities, 27 relations, 17 access records, 22 separately scoped rights records, 3 editorial-practice records, 21 evidence records, and 6 open questions.

The direct digital/documentary path is repository XML -> raw U-M encoding -> U-M TIFFs -> Sieper edition -> Fairfax 16; the separate intellectual relation is whole English work version_of French lines 1-4873.

Complete manuscript objects were cached where possible. Fairfax 16 is present at ff. 202r-300r; the complete SLUB and Harvard French-source objects explicitly record the Middle English target as absent.
The Marciana full-facsimile endpoint returned HTTP 503 on 2026-07-17. This dated access limitation is not modeled as loss or permanent withdrawal.
Rights, licenses, and provider terms remain attached to the exact historical text, edition, encoding, catalog, physical carrier, or reproduction layer supported by evidence.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML is a bounded navigational enrichment of the raw 2006 U-M encoding, which derives from a complete 380-TIFF capture of Ernst Sieper's 1901-1903 EETS edition. Sieper transcribes Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Fairfax 16, ff. 202r-300r, as principal text and collates John Stow's 1558 Add. MS 29729 copy. The one unfinished 7,042-line Middle English work freely translates and expands French lines 1-4,873 of the anonymous Echecs amoureux en vers; the physical French exemplar is unknown, so the three surviving French witnesses remain supporting rather than primary sources.

catalogingSubject.label: Reson and Sensuallyte
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The unfinished 7,042-line Middle English verse translation/adaptation printed on EETS Part I pages 1-184 and encoded in the 104 body sections. Excludes all modern edition prefaces, manuscript descriptions, glossary, studies, notes, indexes, side-note apparatus, omitted front/back matter, page-image navigation, and physical binding.

contentParts.label: Reson and Sensuallyte text
notes: This part is the mutually exclusive representation of the record's one cataloged work, so its item_count is 1 rather than null.
The 104 LG elements are encoded navigational groups, not a proved literary stanza count.
Three note-nested L elements after the poem are excluded from 7,042.
contentParts.summary: The single unfinished Middle English verse work represented by the 104 encoded body sections.

notes: Sieper supports a pre-1412 date and cites Schick's 1406-1408 dating; this is a scholarly estimate, not a documentary date.

extent.description: One unfinished verse work comprising 7,042 poem lines. The 104 XML body sections and 104 LG elements are encoding and navigation groups, not 104 separately cataloged items or a proved literary-stanza count. The sole content part is the mutually exclusive representation of this one work, so its item_count is 1 and reconciles with numbered_items=1.

formStatements.description: An unfinished 7,042-line poem in rhyming couplets; modern prose apparatus is excluded from the work boundary.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The inherited XML header's eng describes the catalog/edition delivery broadly; the medieval work text is Middle English. French source text and edition-only Latin/French belong to other layers.
The record describes one unfinished medieval work, not the whole two-volume Sieper edition and not a compilation.
The sole content part is the mutually exclusive representation of that one work, so item_count=1 and aggregate numbered_items=1.
The 104 XML body sections and LG elements are encoding/navigation groups, not independently counted works or proved literary stanzas.
England is retained only as a tentative broad work-level cultural context; exact composition place remains unresolved and witness production locations are not transferred to the work.

next_steps: Retain attributed translator and adapter responsibilities at medium confidence.
Review new attribution scholarship or manuscript evidence before changing the responsibility status.
openQuestions.question: Can future scholarship raise or lower the qualified Lydgate attribution?

next_steps: Keep the source relation at the abstract-work level.
Do not place a surviving French witness in the primary transmission path without direct exemplar evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical French exemplar, if any extant, did the English translator use?

notes: England is a qualified broad cultural-context inference from work/version-level scholarship on the Middle English translation, not a proved place of composition.
Exact place of composition is unresolved; do not infer Bury St Edmunds from attribution and do not use the production origins of Fairfax 16 or Add. 29729 as composition evidence.

subjects.label: Chess, courtly love, and the allegorical game of love
Conflict and accord between reason and sensuality
Classical mythology and personified faculties, including Nature, Venus, Diana, and Reason
Moral instruction concerning virtue, sensual appetite, and love

summaries.value: An unfinished Middle English dream-vision allegory, cautiously attributed to John Lydgate as translator-adapter, which freely expands the opening 4,873 lines of the anonymous French Echecs amoureux into 7,042 rhyming-couplet lines. Nature, Venus, Diana, personified faculties, the garden of love, and an allegorical chess game frame instruction about reason, sensual appetite, virtue, and courtly love.

The metadata record covers only the unfinished medieval English poem. All modern edition apparatus, page-image navigation, catalog descriptions, physical carriers, French source witnesses, and repository encodings are lineage layers rather than additional work parts.

notes: Modernized spelling supplied for discovery, not asserted as an original title.
The source-edition title; the possessive does not remove the qualified medieval attribution.
